findings.summary: generate_pipeline_report and generate_validation_report carry full Args/Returns docstrings that restate the parameter names and types already visible in the signature
evidence: generate_validation_report docstring: 'validation_results: Validation results dictionary' — name restates type
generate_performance_report docstring: 'output_dir: Output directory path' — identical to parameter name
generate_error_summary docstring: 'errors: List of error dictionaries' — zero information beyond the type hint
findings.suggestion: Keep only the docstring sentence that says what the function decides or enforces (e.g. 'Writes JSON + Markdown side by side'). Drop Args/Returns blocks whose content is already expressed by the type annotations.

findings.summary: CodeProfiler.profile_function logs to INFO and prints cProfile stats for every profiled call, turning routine profiling into console noise
evidence: Line 426: logger.info(f'Profile results for {func.__name__}:')
Line 427: stats.print_stats(10) — goes to stdout unconditionally
monitor() already logs at DEBUG after the change; profile_function is inconsistent and noisier
findings.suggestion: Drop the logger.info call and move stats output to DEBUG or return the Stats object so callers decide whether to print.

findings.summary: Several short utility functions in suite_summary_generator.py carry three-section docstrings (summary + Args + Returns) for trivial one-liners
evidence: _calculate_weighted_coverage: one-liner weighted average; carries full docstring with Returns block
_aggregate_counts: eight-line arithmetic function with full Args/Returns docstring
_is_ollama_available: two lines, single importlib call; full docstring with Returns
findings.suggestion: Collapse to single-line docstrings for private helpers. The function names already communicate intent.

findings.summary: coverage_parser.py contains restating inline comments that echo the conditional logic
evidence: Line 38 comment: '# If we can't check, assume it's not supported to be safe' — the except branch returning False already communicates this
Line 291: '# Fallback to stdout parsing' — the next line is a regex on stdout_text; the comment adds nothing
findings.suggestion: Remove comments that narrate what the next line literally does. Keep comments that explain why (e.g. 'pytest-cov < 4.0 does not expose --cov-datafile').

findings.summary: benchmark_function exists as two independent implementations with overlapping purpose: infrastructure.scientific.benchmarking and CodeProfiler.benchmark_function in infrastructure.core.performance
evidence: infrastructure/scientific/benchmarking.py:37 defines benchmark_function(func, test_inputs, iterations) returning BenchmarkResult
infrastructure/core/performance.py:429 defines CodeProfiler.benchmark_function(func, *args, iterations, warmup_iterations) returning Dict
Both measure wall-clock time of repeated function calls with warmup; different signatures, different return types, same conceptual job
All callers in tests and production code use the scientific version; the CodeProfiler version has one test in test_performance_monitor.py
findings.suggestion: Remove or delegate CodeProfiler.benchmark_function to infrastructure.scientific.benchmarking.benchmark_function, or document the distinct use-case (e.g. call-level profiling with pstats vs input-parameterized scientific benchmarking).

findings.summary: ReportingStageResult inherits from checkpoint.StageResult to add three list fields, creating a cross-layer inheritance dependency for a trivial extension
evidence: infrastructure/reporting/pipeline_reporter.py:22: class ReportingStageResult(_CheckpointStageResult)
The three added fields (output_files, errors, warnings) are never populated in generate_pipeline_report — they stay as empty lists
checkpoint.StageResult is a core data class; reporting pulling it into inheritance couples the reporting layer to the core checkpoint data model
findings.suggestion: Use composition instead: store a CheckpointStageResult inside ReportingStageResult, or simply add the three fields directly to a standalone reporting dataclass that mirrors only the fields it needs.

findings.summary: generate_pipeline_report immediately unpacks every field of ReportExtras into local variables, making the ReportExtras abstraction invisible one function later
evidence: Lines 78-83: six consecutive 'x = extras.x if extras is not None else None' assignments
The unpacked variables are used directly rather than extras.field, making extras a pass-through with no policy
findings.suggestion: Either use extras fields directly (extras.test_results if extras else None) at each call site, or accept them as **kwargs. The current pattern defeats the purpose of the dataclass grouping.

findings.summary: Tests import generate_multi_project_summary_report from pipeline_reporter, but the function lives in multi_project_reporter
evidence: tests/infra_tests/reporting/test_pipeline_reporter.py:361: from infrastructure.reporting.pipeline_reporter import generate_multi_project_summary_report
grep confirms generate_multi_project_summary_report is defined only in infrastructure/reporting/multi_project_reporter.py:65
This import will raise ImportError at runtime unless pipeline_reporter re-exports it (which it does not based on reading the full file)
findings.suggestion: Move the test import to multi_project_reporter, or add a deliberate re-export in pipeline_reporter with a comment noting the delegation.

findings.summary: format_duration is imported from both logging_helpers (canonical) and logging_utils (re-export), creating two valid import paths for the same symbol
evidence: logging_progress.py imports from logging_helpers directly
performance.py and pipeline_reporter.py and pipeline_summary.py import from logging_utils
logging_utils re-exports format_duration via 'from infrastructure.core.logging_helpers import format_duration'
Both paths resolve to the same object but the inconsistency means a refactor of logging_utils breaks callers who expected a stable re-export
findings.suggestion: Pick one canonical source (logging_helpers) and have all callers import from there, or explicitly list format_duration in logging_utils.__all__ with a note that it is a stable re-export.

findings.summary: _PROCESS_START_TIME is assigned at module import time in health_check.py, which is a side effect at import for a module used as an optional health monitor
evidence: Line 276: _PROCESS_START_TIME: float = time.time() — executes on first import
This is documented as 'zero side effects — just reads the clock' but any module-level time.time() call is a side effect visible to profilers and test isolation
The lazy _get_checker() pattern was added for the checker itself but not for the timestamp it depends on
findings.suggestion: This is a low-severity issue given the comment, but if strict import-time neutrality is desired, initialize _PROCESS_START_TIME inside _get_checker() on first call rather than at module scope.

findings.summary: Three distinct StageResult-like classes exist with overlapping names: checkpoint.StageResult, pipeline.PipelineStageResult, and reporting.ReportingStageResult — no single naming convention
evidence: checkpoint.StageResult: 6 fields (name, exit_code, duration, timestamp, completed, status)
pipeline.PipelineStageResult: positional dataclass with stage_number, name, success, duration — different field set
reporting.ReportingStageResult: inherits checkpoint.StageResult and adds output_files, errors, warnings
These three types serve overlapping purposes but have incompatible fields and different naming patterns (no prefix, Pipeline prefix, Reporting prefix)
findings.suggestion: Establish one canonical stage-result type in core and layer extensions via composition, not multiple near-duplicate dataclasses. Or at minimum, align field names (exit_code vs success is the most confusing divergence).

findings.summary: Private parse helper functions in coverage_parser.py inconsistently have or omit docstrings — some have full one-liners, others bare signatures
evidence: _parse_failures_section: has docstring 'Parse failures from the FAILURES section (--tb=line format).'
_parse_failures_verbose: has docstring
_parse_failures_timeout: has docstring
_wait_for_coverage_file: has docstring
_parse_coverage_json: has docstring
Pattern is consistent here — this is actually a positive example, flagged only for contrast with health_check.py private methods which were recently stripped
findings.suggestion: The coverage_parser pattern (one-line docstrings on private helpers) is the correct convention. Ensure health_check.py private methods without docstrings (_check_memory, _check_cpu, _check_disk, _check_network, _check_dependencies) follow the same pattern with at minimum a one-liner.

findings.summary: logging_utils.py has a mid-file import with noqa: E402 that violates the standard top-of-file import convention used everywhere else
evidence: Line 80: from infrastructure.core.logging_constants import EMOJIS, USE_EMOJIS, USE_STRUCTURED_LOGGING  # noqa: E402
This import appears after function definitions and section comments, suppressing the linter rather than restructuring
All other infrastructure modules import at the top of the file
findings.suggestion: Move this import to the top of the file with the other infrastructure.core imports. If a circular import prevented this, the recent logging_constants split should have already resolved it — verify and clean up.

findings.summary: Two parallel StageResult types exist at different layers with overlapping responsibility
evidence: checkpoint.py defines StageResult with fields: name, exit_code, duration, timestamp, completed, status
pipeline.py defines PipelineStageResult with fields: stage_num, stage_name, success, duration, exit_code, error_message, exception_type
pipeline_reporter.py defines ReportingStageResult(CheckpointStageResult) adding output_files, errors, warnings — a third variant
pipeline.py imports and uses CheckpointManager.StageResult internally but returns PipelineStageResult to callers, requiring manual translation between them
findings.suggestion: Decide on a single canonical result type per layer boundary. If pipeline.py already wraps checkpoint.py, PipelineStageResult should be that type's public face; drop the checkpoint.StageResult from pipeline's public API surface, or make ReportingStageResult extend PipelineStageResult instead of CheckpointStageResult.

findings.summary: performance.py intentionally hosts two unrelated sub-domains in one file to avoid circular imports
evidence: Module docstring explicitly states: 'Sub-domain 1 — Stage monitoring (psutil-based, pipeline-level)' and 'Sub-domain 2 — Function profiling (cProfile/tracemalloc, call-level)'
Comment says 'Both sub-domains share this module intentionally to avoid circular imports' with a suggestion to split later
PerformanceMonitor (stage-level) and CodeProfiler (function-level) are entirely independent classes with no shared state or logic
findings.suggestion: The circular-import avoidance rationale is a workaround, not a design decision. Introduce an infrastructure/core/_optional_deps-style shim or restructure the import graph so stage_monitor.py and function_profiler.py can exist independently without cycles.

findings.summary: generate_review_with_metrics has 9 positional/keyword parameters where a config object would reduce coupling
evidence: Signature: generate_review_with_metrics(client, text, review_type, review_name, template_class, model_name='', temperature=0.3, max_tokens=None, max_retries=1)
review_type and review_name are always paired together; template_class, temperature, max_tokens and max_retries are all review-configuration concerns
The function body immediately unpacks these into a local GenerationOptions — the parameter bag pattern identified in holistic_context wide_param_bags
findings.suggestion: Introduce a ReviewConfig dataclass grouping review_type, review_name, template_class, temperature, max_tokens, max_retries. Callers pass one object instead of 6 scalars.

findings.summary: I/O boundary functions inconsistently return None vs raise on failure across the reporting layer
evidence: suite_summary_generator.load_test_results() catches (OSError, json.JSONDecodeError) and returns {} — callers cannot distinguish missing file from parse error
suite_summary_generator.run_test_summary_generation() catches OSError on write and then re-raises — different recovery posture from the read side
coverage_parser._parse_coverage_json() returns None on json.JSONDecodeError or OSError — callers get None with no error context
pipeline_reporter.generate_pipeline_report() is pure and raises nothing — consistent within itself but forces callers to accept silent data loss from the loading functions above it
findings.suggestion: Pick one strategy per boundary type: either return a typed Result/sentinel with an attached reason field, or raise a specific TemplateError subclass. The current mix (empty dict, None, re-raise) forces each caller to guess which failure mode they got.

findings.summary: generator.py line 642 catches Exception in the retry loop and continues without logging the exception
evidence: Lines 642-644: 'except Exception as e: if attempt < max_retries: continue' — the exception variable e is referenced in the condition guard but never logged when attempt < max_retries
This is the path for all retried LLM failures; if Ollama returns a transient error on attempt 0, it is silently discarded
Contrast with line 800 in the same file which does log the error: 'logger.debug(f"Streaming query failed, falling back: {e}")'
findings.suggestion: Add logger.debug(f'Review attempt {attempt} failed: {e}, retrying') before the continue. The recent fix added logging to 3 bare except blocks, but this retry-path exception was missed.

findings.summary: repo_scanner.py line 247 uses bare except: return False, swallowing unknown exceptions silently
evidence: Line 247-248: 'except Exception: return False' — no exception variable bound, no logging
This is inside a function that validates repository structure; a spurious AttributeError or NameError would be silently treated as a validation failure
Surrounding catches in the same file (e.g. lines 220-221, 310-311) all bind the exception and log at debug level — this one is an outlier
findings.suggestion: Change to 'except Exception as e: logger.debug("Unexpected error in repo_scanner: %s", e); return False' to match the surrounding pattern.

findings.summary: scientific/templates.py has two bare 'except Exception: pass' blocks that silently discard failures
evidence: Lines 160 and 178: 'except Exception: pass' — exception not bound, not logged
These are in template rendering paths where silent failure could produce subtly incorrect scientific output with no diagnostic trace
findings.suggestion: Replace with 'except Exception as e: logger.debug("Template step failed: %s", e)' to preserve the tolerance-for-failure posture while at least leaving a trace.

findings.summary: infrastructure/llm/templates/base.py and manuscript.py are critical (3 importers each) but have no dedicated test coverage
evidence: holistic_context.testing.critical_untested lists both files with importers=3
base.py defines ResearchTemplate.render() which is the single template execution path for all LLM reviews; its KeyError-to-LLMTemplateError conversion is untested
manuscript.py defines all five review template classes (ManuscriptExecutiveSummary etc.) and REVIEW_MIN_WORDS constants used in quality validation — no test file targets these classes directly
test_metrics.py was added (new file per git status) but covers only ReviewMetrics dataclasses, not templates
findings.suggestion: Add tests/infra_tests/llm/test_templates.py that exercises ResearchTemplate.render() with valid and missing variables, and spot-checks at least two ManuscriptXxx template classes to confirm they produce non-empty strings with expected section headers.

findings.summary: 78 test files import mock-related names, violating the codebase's explicit no-mocks policy
evidence: Grep for 'mock|Mock|MagicMock|mocker.patch|patch(' across tests/ returns 78 files with matches
CLAUDE.md states: 'ABSOLUTE REQUIREMENT: Never use MagicMock, mocker.patch, unittest.mock, or any mocking framework'
Many hits include 'mocker' fixture and 'mock' imports alongside real-data tests — some may be in comments, conftest fixtures, or monkeypatching rather than true MagicMock usage, reducing certainty
findings.suggestion: Audit the 78 files to distinguish monkeypatch (permitted pytest fixture) from unittest.mock / MagicMock (prohibited). Replace any true MagicMock usage with real objects, pytest-httpserver for HTTP, or real temp file fixtures. The policy exists to prevent fragile coupling to implementation internals.

findings.summary: suite_summary_generator.run_test_summary_generation() has no test coverage for its OSError re-raise branch
evidence: run_test_summary_generation() writes two files (JSON and markdown) and re-raises OSError on write failure
test_summary_generator.py tests load_test_results, load_infrastructure_results, generate_summary_report, and generate_markdown_report — but not run_test_summary_generation()
The write-error re-raise path is the only function that exits non-zero from the reporting layer; leaving it untested means the pipeline error signal is unverified
findings.suggestion: Add a test that calls run_test_summary_generation() in a tmp_path with write-protected output to verify the OSError propagates, and a success-path test that checks both output files are created.
